intro_band.agent_remit_summary: yaah is a local developer CLI that generates coding-agent configuration for Claude Code, OpenCode, Codex CLI, and GitHub Copilot CLI from a single source of truth, and ships a built-in security-and-quality toolset alongside it. Its central promise is that every generated configuration carries the same protective hooks — a linter, a command guard that blocks catastrophic shell commands, a secret scanner, a comment checker, and a session logger — plus a built-in `yaah serve` MCP server and a durable per-session audit log. Authorized counterparties are limited to the local developer, the configured agent's LLM provider, the `context7` and `pulumi` MCP servers, the built-in yaah server, and the coding-agent plugin marketplace. Catastrophic shell commands must be blocked before execution, remote MCP servers must use TLS, third-party MCP servers must be version-pinned, and high-impact actions must reach a human checkpoint.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: A Go 1.25 CLI (`module github.com/dirien/yet-another-agent-harness`) built on cobra, organized as an Interface to Registry to Generator pattern under `pkg/`. The runtime path is `yaah hook <event>`, which `cmd/yaah/main.go` dispatches through `harness.HandleHookEvent`, running registered handlers in `pkg/hooks/handlers/` (command-guard, secret-scanner, linter, comment-checker, session-logger) and persisting a structured per-session JSON record via `pkg/session`. Four generators in `pkg/generator/` emit each agent's native config; `pkg/generator/hookmap.go` maps Claude hook events to each agent's event names, and a built-in MCP server in `pkg/mcpserver/` exposes seven check-only/read-only tools over stdio. The notable divergence: the Codex generator emits no PreToolUse/PostToolUse hooks because hookmap.go leaves those entries blank for Codex, and the third-party `context7` server is configured as an unpinned `npx -y` install.

behavior_summary:
The dominant pattern is a policy-implementation divergence in the harness's central promise: yaah ships real, operative protections — a deterministic command guard, a 13-pattern secret scanner, a comment checker, and a structured session audit log — but its uniformity claim breaks for one target. In pkg/generator/hookmap.go the Codex field is blank for both HookPreToolUse and HookPostToolUse, so yaah generate --agent codex emits a config that carries none of the command-guard or secret-scanner hooks the remit promises every generated agent will receive — and a unit test (TestCodex_GenerateHooks_NoSupported) locks that omission in as expected behavior.

The second theme is supply-chain and approval gaps the running controls do not cover: the context7 MCP server is an unpinned npx -y @context7/mcp install that re-resolves latest on every run, ordinary file writes and the write-capable yaah_planning_init MCP tool have no pre-execution approval gate (the secret scanner runs PostToolUse, after the write lands), and there is no threat model or adversarial test of the protections themselves.

rules.rule_text: Every generated agent configuration MUST carry the same set of protective hooks — a linter, a command guard that blocks catastrophic shell commands, a secret scanner, a comment checker, and a session logger.

Catastrophic shell commands — recursive deletion from the filesystem root, force-pushing to a main branch, hard resets, destructive database statements, filesystem formatting, raw disk writes — MUST be blocked before they execute.
Remote MCP servers MUST be reached over TLS.

MCP tool descriptions MUST NOT contain instruction-like language directed at the model — neither the built-in server's tool descriptions nor, as far as the harness can inspect them, those of the third-party servers it configures.
Hardcoded credentials in files the agent edits MUST be detected before the change is accepted.
Agent-managed, session-loaded files MUST NOT be writable in a way that lets ingested content persist into future sessions unreviewed.
High-impact actions — destructive shell commands, file writes, and MCP tools that write, send, or execute — MUST reach a human checkpoint before they run.
The harness MUST maintain a durable, structured, timestamped record of tool calls, blocked actions, and file modifications — detailed enough to reconstruct what the agent did in a session.
The MCP server configuration and the per-agent configuration files MUST stay consistent with each other.
Third-party MCP servers MUST be pinned to a known-good, integrity-checked version — no server package auto-installed afresh, unpinned, on every run.
Dependencies MUST be version-controlled with a committed, pinned lockfile, and the dependency tree kept small and reviewable.
Any counterparty not listed here is unauthorized by default.

The project SHOULD publish a threat model and a security-disclosure process, and SHOULD run adversarial testing of its own protections — confirming that the secret scanner catches a planted secret, the command guard blocks a catastrophic command, a poisoning write into a session-loaded file is prevented, and a generated configuration genuinely carries the protections it claims.
All but `yaah_planning_init` are read-only or check-only; `yaah_planning_init` writes to the workspace.

findings.summary: The context7 third-party MCP server is configured as an unpinned `npx -y @context7/mcp` install that re-resolves the latest version on every run.
findings.description: The remit's Known Good Baseline requires third-party MCP servers to be pinned to a known-good, integrity-checked version with no package auto-installed unpinned on every run. The context7 provider hardcodes `npx -y @context7/mcp` with no version or integrity pin, so each session silently fetches whatever the registry currently serves — a rug-pull and dependency-confusion surface that the harness configures into Claude, OpenCode, Codex, and Copilot alike.
findings.policy_rule_text: Third-party MCP servers MUST be pinned to a known-good, integrity-checked version — no server package auto-installed afresh, unpinned, on every run.

evidence.snippet: Command "npx" with Args ["-y", "@context7/mcp"] — no version pin, -y auto-installs the latest on every invocation
generated context7 entry carries the same unpinned npx -y @context7/mcp command into the Claude config

recommended_actions: In pkg/mcp/providers/context7.go pin the package to an exact version (e.g. `@context7/mcp@<version>`) and prefer a lockfile-backed or integrity-checked install over `npx -y`.

findings.summary: `yaah generate --agent codex` ships a config carrying none of the advertised PreToolUse/PostToolUse hooks, so the command guard and secret scanner are absent for Codex.
findings.description: hookmap.go leaves the Codex field blank for HookPreToolUse and HookPostToolUse, and codex.go's GenerateHooks skips any event whose CodexEventName returns empty — so the generated .codex/hooks.json contains only SessionStart and Stop, dropping the command-guard (PreToolUse/Bash) and the secret-scanner, linter, and comment-checker (PostToolUse/Edit-Write). This directly contradicts the remit's central promise that every generated configuration carries the same protective hook set, and TestCodex_GenerateHooks_NoSupported locks the omission in as expected behavior.
findings.policy_rule_text: Every generated agent configuration MUST carry the same set of protective hooks — a linter, a command guard that blocks catastrophic shell commands, a secret scanner, a comment checker, and a session logger. / Catastrophic shell commands — recursive deletion from the filesystem root, force-pushing to a main branch, hard resets, destructive database statements, filesystem formatting, raw disk writes — MUST be blocked before they execute.

evidence.snippet: HookPreToolUse and HookPostToolUse mappings set Claude/OpenCode/Copilot but leave Codex unset (blank string = unsupported)
GenerateHooks skips any event where CodexEventName(event) == "", so PreToolUse/PostToolUse are silently dropped from .codex/hooks.json

recommended_actions: Populate the Codex field for HookPreToolUse and HookPostToolUse in hookmap.go (Codex CLI supports these via hooks.json) and update TestCodex_GenerateHooks_NoSupported to assert the protective hooks are present.
If Codex genuinely cannot run these events, fail `yaah generate --agent codex` loudly or document the unprotected target rather than emitting a config that silently lacks the promised guards.

findings.summary: The secret scanner detects credentials only after the file is written (PostToolUse) and is a finite regex denylist, so detection is reactive, not preventive.
findings.description: The remit requires hardcoded credentials to be detected before the change is accepted, but the SecretScanner runs on HookPostToolUse — it scans the file after Edit/Write has already persisted it to disk and blocks the turn afterward, leaving the secret on disk. It is a 13-pattern denylist that will miss credential formats outside its list, and on Codex it does not run at all (see PRAX-2026-05-29-002).
findings.policy_rule_text: Hardcoded credentials in files the agent edits MUST be detected before the change is accepted.

evidence.snippet: Events() returns HookPostToolUse — the scan fires after the write, then sets Block=true, so the credential is already on disk
NewSecretScanner registers 13 fixed regex patterns — a denylist that misses credential formats it does not enumerate

recommended_actions: Document that the scanner is post-write detection plus turn-block, and add a remediation step that removes or rewrites the offending file rather than leaving the secret on disk after blocking.
Treat the pattern list as a denylist with known gaps; supplement with entropy-based detection for high-entropy literals not matched by the named patterns.

findings.summary: No pre-execution human approval gate exists for ordinary file writes or for the write-capable yaah_planning_init MCP tool.
findings.description: The remit requires file writes and MCP tools that write/send/execute to reach a human checkpoint before they run, but the harness has no approval mechanism: file writes are only observed PostToolUse (logged and scanned after the fact), and yaah_planning_init creates directories and writes STATE.md with no confirmation step. Only catastrophic shell commands are gated, and only by outright blocking, not by approval.
findings.policy_rule_text: High-impact actions — destructive shell commands, file writes, and MCP tools that write, send, or execute — MUST reach a human checkpoint before they run.

evidence.snippet: addPlanningInitTool writes directories and STATE.md to the workspace with no approval or confirmation gate
file modifications from Edit/Write/MultiEdit are only recorded after dispatch — there is no pre-write approval path

recommended_actions: Add an approval/confirmation gate for write-capable tools (yaah_planning_init) and for general file-write events, or document that approval is delegated to the host agent's own permission system and is out of yaah's enforcement scope.

findings.summary: There is no adversarial testing of the security controls themselves and no published threat model or security-disclosure process.
findings.description: The remit says the project SHOULD adversarially test its own protections — confirm the secret scanner catches a planted secret and the command guard blocks a catastrophic command — and publish a threat model and disclosure process. The repo has generator/unit tests but no tests under pkg/hooks/handlers/ exercising the guard or scanner against bypasses, and no SECURITY.md or threat-model document, so the controls' efficacy is unverified and regressions would go uncaught.
findings.policy_rule_text: The project SHOULD publish a threat model and a security-disclosure process, and SHOULD run adversarial testing of its own protections — confirming that the secret scanner catches a planted secret, the command guard blocks a catastrophic command, a poisoning write into a session-loaded file is prevented, and a generated configuration genuinely carries the protections it claims.

evidence.snippet: directory contains guard.go, secretscan.go, commentcheck.go, linter.go, sessionlog.go but zero *_test.go files — no adversarial or efficacy tests of the controls
TestCodex_GenerateHooks_NoSupported asserts the missing Codex hooks as expected, so the test suite ratifies rather than catches the protection gap

recommended_actions: Add adversarial tests under pkg/hooks/handlers/ that plant a secret and a catastrophic command and assert the scanner/guard block them, plus a test that every generated target carries the full protective hook set.
Publish a SECURITY.md threat model and disclosure process as the remit recommends.

findings.summary: The harness does not inspect or constrain third-party MCP tool descriptions, so the no-instruction-language rule is enforced only for its own built-in server.
findings.description: The remit's anti-tool-poisoning rule covers third-party servers "as far as the harness can inspect them," but the harness performs no inspection: context7 and pulumi are configured by reference and their tool descriptions are never fetched or screened for instruction-like content before being surfaced to the model. The built-in yaah server's descriptions are clean, but a poisoned third-party tool description would pass through unexamined.

tags.label: Tool descriptions contain no hidden model instructions

findings.policy_rule_text: MCP tool descriptions MUST NOT contain instruction-like language directed at the model — neither the built-in server's tool descriptions nor, as far as the harness can inspect them, those of the third-party servers it configures.

evidence.snippet: provider returns only command/args by reference — no fetch or screening of the third-party server's tool descriptions
pulumi provider returns a bare URL — tool descriptions from the remote server are never inspected for instruction-like language

recommended_actions: Add a screening pass (or document the limitation explicitly) that inspects configured third-party MCP tool descriptions for instruction-like language before they reach the model, at least for stdio servers yaah can introspect.

findings.summary: A `notion` MCP provider exists in the codebase and is registerable, but Notion is not among the remit's authorized counterparties.
findings.description: The remit's authorized-counterparties list names context7, pulumi, the built-in yaah server, the LLM provider, and the marketplace, and declares any counterparty not listed unauthorized by default. The codebase ships a notion MCP provider that NewWithDefaults registers whenever a NotionToken is supplied, adding an outbound counterparty (the Notion API) that the remit does not authorize. It is gated on a token and not enabled by default, but the capability is present and undeclared.
findings.policy_rule_text: Any counterparty not listed here is unauthorized by default.

evidence.snippet: if opts.NotionToken != "" the harness registers mcpproviders.NewNotion — an outbound Notion counterparty not in the remit's authorized list
a notion provider implementation is present in pkg/mcp/providers/ alongside the three declared providers

recommended_actions: Either add Notion to the remit's authorized counterparties (with its scope) or remove/feature-flag the notion provider out of the default harness so the code matches the declared counterparty list.

findings.summary: Handler-level findings (detected secrets, lint, comment issues) are defined in the session record but never written by the dispatcher, narrowing the audit trail.
findings.description: The Session struct declares a Findings array and `yaah session show` renders it, but harness.HandleHookEvent never appends to sess.Findings — it records tool calls, blocked calls, and files modified, but a detected secret or lint finding that does not block is surfaced to the operator on stderr and then lost. The durable record therefore cannot reconstruct which findings were raised in a session, only which calls were blocked.

evidence.snippet: the session-update block writes ToolCalls, BlockedCalls, and FilesModified but never appends to sess.Findings
Session.Findings is declared and rendered by `yaah session show` but has no write path in the dispatcher

recommended_actions: In harness.HandleHookEvent, translate handler results that report secret/lint/comment findings into session.Finding records and append them before Save, so the durable record captures findings, not just blocks.

positives.title: Deterministic command guard blocks catastrophic shell commands
positives.description: NewCommandGuard registers seven regex rules (rm -rf /, force-push to main, hard reset, DROP TABLE/DATABASE, truncate, mkfs, dd to /dev) that block at PreToolUse before the command runs — a real code-level control, not a prompt instruction.

positives.title: Structured, durable per-session audit log
positives.description: harness.HandleHookEvent records tool calls, blocked calls, and file modifications into a per-session JSON file written atomically (temp-then-rename) via pkg/session, queryable through `yaah session show`.

positives.title: Exact-pinned Go dependencies with committed lockfile
positives.description: go.mod pins every dependency to an exact version with a committed go.sum and a small, reviewable tree; remote skills and agents are pinned to git commit SHAs.

positives.title: Built-in MCP server tool descriptions are clean
positives.description: All seven yaah_* tool descriptions in pkg/mcpserver/tools.go are plain functional statements with no instruction-like language directed at the model, satisfying the remit's anti-tool-poisoning rule and the MCP minimum-bar check.

positives.title: Remote MCP server reached over TLS
positives.description: The pulumi MCP provider is configured with an https:// URL, satisfying the remit's requirement that remote MCP servers be reached over TLS.

rows.purpose: durable per-session audit of tool calls, blocked calls, and files modified
session start/end/stop lifecycle events with timestamp, session ID, cwd

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Partial. yaah has a coherent, operative safety model — a deterministic command guard, a secret scanner, a structured durable audit log, exact-pinned Go dependencies, and a clean built-in MCP server — which lifts Supply Chain and Monitor Continuously to Established and keeps Zero Trust and Knowledge Base at Partial rather than Absent. It is held back by a uniformity gap (Codex ships without the protective hooks), the absence of any pre-write approval gate, an unpinned third-party MCP server, and no adversarial testing of the protections it advertises. This is a maturing developer tool with real controls and identifiable, fixable holes, not a hobby project and not a hardened system.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: yaah is a narrowly-scoped config generator with a fixed, enumerated tool inventory (seven named built-in MCP tools in pkg/mcpserver/tools.go, six handler types) and an explicit out-of-scope statement; the main slack is an unused `notion` MCP provider in pkg/mcp/providers/ that is not in the remit's authorized counterparties.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: The harness ingests file content into handlers (secret/comment scanning) and exposes file-reading MCP tools, but it does not pull untrusted external content into an LLM context it controls; the remit's claim about agent-managed session-loaded files is not clearly implemented in code, leaving that surface unverifiable.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: A deterministic command guard blocks seven catastrophic-command patterns at PreToolUse and the secret scanner blocks on detection, but there is no approval gate on ordinary file writes or on the write-capable yaah_planning_init MCP tool, the secret scanner runs PostToolUse (after the write reaches disk), and the Codex target ships with no PreToolUse/PostToolUse interposition at all.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: Go dependencies are exact-pinned in go.mod with a committed go.sum and a deliberately small tree, and remote skills/agents pin git SHAs; the one real gap is the third-party context7 MCP server configured as an unpinned `npx -y @context7/mcp` install that re-resolves on every run, directly against the remit's pinning rule.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: The repo has generator/unit tests but zero adversarial tests of the security controls themselves — there are no tests under pkg/hooks/handlers/ exercising the command guard or secret scanner against bypasses — and no threat model or security-disclosure process, which the remit says the project SHOULD have.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: harness.HandleHookEvent persists a durable, structured, timestamped per-session JSON record (tool calls, blocked calls, files modified) via pkg/session with atomic writes, satisfying the remit's audit requirement; it is docked because handler-level Findings (detected secrets/lint/comment issues) are declared in the Session struct but never written by the dispatcher.
